quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Tension"?

options.text: The pulling force transmitted through a string, rope, or cable; in an ideal (massless) string, tension is the same throughout
W_nc = ΔKE + ΔPE = E_final - E_initial; work by friction or applied forces that changes the total mechanical energy
1 hp = 746 W; a unit of power commonly used for engines and motors
A dimensionless vector of magnitude 1 indicating direction; î, ĵ, k̂ denote the x, y, z directions respectively

quiz_questions.explanation: "Tension" is defined as: The pulling force transmitted through a string, rope, or cable; in an ideal (massless) string, tension is the same throughout. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Normal force"?

options.text: The contact force perpendicular to a surface, adjusting to prevent objects from passing through each other
A quantity with both magnitude and direction, represented as an arrow; examples include displacement, velocity, force, and momentum
F_ext,net = M_total × a_cm; the center of mass accelerates as if all mass and net external force were concentrated there
W = ∫F·dr; the scalar integral of force along displacement; measured in joules (J); positive when force and displacement are in the same direction

quiz_questions.explanation: "Normal force" is defined as: The contact force perpendicular to a surface, adjusting to prevent objects from passing through each other. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Weight"?

options.text: 1 hp = 746 W; a unit of power commonly used for engines and motors
The vector sum of all forces acting on an object; determines the direction and magnitude of acceleration via F_net = ma
The change in position vector Δx = x_final - x_initial; a signed quantity indicating direction, measured in meters
The gravitational force W = mg directed toward Earth's center; a body force, not a contact force

quiz_questions.explanation: "Weight" is defined as: The gravitational force W = mg directed toward Earth's center; a body force, not a contact force. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "System of equations"?

options.text: The energy required to completely separate a system; equals |E_total| for a gravitationally bound system
F_avg = Δp/Δt = J/Δt; the constant force that would deliver the same impulse over the same time interval
p = mv; a vector quantity equal to mass times velocity, measured in kg·m/s
Multiple F = ma equations (one per object or direction) solved simultaneously for unknowns like acceleration or tension

quiz_questions.explanation: "System of equations" is defined as: Multiple F = ma equations (one per object or direction) solved simultaneously for unknowns like acceleration or tension. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Atwood machine"?

options.text: A system of two masses connected by a string over a pulley; a = (m₁ - m₂)g/(m₁ + m₂) for an ideal massless pulley
a(t) = dv/dt = d²x/dt²; the time derivative of velocity, indicating how quickly velocity changes
F_net = dp/dt; the net force equals the time rate of change of momentum
v_esc = √(2GM/r); the minimum speed needed to escape a gravitational field entirely (total energy = 0)

quiz_questions.explanation: "Atwood machine" is defined as: A system of two masses connected by a string over a pulley; a = (m₁ - m₂)g/(m₁ + m₂) for an ideal massless pulley. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The pulling force transmitted through a string, rope, or cable; in an ideal (massless) string, tension is the same throughout"?

options.text: Superposition principle
Center of mass reference frame
Power
Tension

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Tension".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The contact force perpendicular to a surface, adjusting to prevent objects from passing through each other"?

options.text: Static equilibrium
Kepler's Third Law
Coefficient of friction
Normal force

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Normal force".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The gravitational force W = mg directed toward Earth's center; a body force, not a contact force"?

options.text: Work by a spring
Non-uniform circular motion
Gravitational field
Weight

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Weight".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Multiple F = ma equations (one per object or direction) solved simultaneously for unknowns like acceleration or tension"?

options.text: Unit vector
Superposition principle
Power
System of equations

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "System of equations".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A system of two masses connected by a string over a pulley; a = (m₁ - m₂)g/(m₁ + m₂) for an ideal massless pulley"?

options.text: Conservation of angular momentum
Work by a spring
Horsepower
Atwood machine

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Atwood machine".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Forces and Motion is accurate?

options.text: Power is defined as P = dW/dt = F·v (in watts), connecting force, velocity, and energy transfer rate.
Forces and Motion applies Newton's laws to systems with multiple forces, including tension, normal force, applied forces, and weight.
Vectors and Components provide the mathematical language for describing physical quantities with both magnitude and direction.
Force-potential energy diagrams reveal equilibrium positions (where F = -dU/dx = 0) and their stability (slope determines restoring or repelling character).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Forces and Motion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Forces and Motion?

options.text: Force-potential energy diagrams reveal equilibrium positions (where F = -dU/dx = 0) and their stability (slope determines restoring or repelling character).
Systematic problem-solving involves drawing free-body diagrams, choosing a coordinate system, applying ΣF = ma in each direction, and solving the resulting system of equations.
The physical (compound) pendulum accounts for distributed mass: T = 2π√(I/(mgd)), where I is the moment of inertia about the pivot and d is the distance from pivot to center of mass.
Work and Kinetic Energy defines work rigorously using calculus: W = ∫F·dr, the line integral of force along the displacement path.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Forces and Motion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Forces and Motion?

options.text: Work and Energy provide a scalar framework for analyzing motion without tracking forces and accelerations over time.
The impulse-momentum theorem (J = ∫F dt = Δp) connects the time integral of force to the change in momentum, making it especially useful for analyzing collisions and impacts.
Connected-body problems (Atwood machines, blocks on inclines connected by strings) require writing F = ma for each object and using constraint equations.
Vectors and Components provide the mathematical language for describing physical quantities with both magnitude and direction.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Forces and Motion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Forces and Motion?

options.text: Forces and Motion applies Newton's laws to systems with multiple forces, including tension, normal force, applied forces, and weight.
The center of mass moves as if all the system's mass were concentrated there with F_ext = Ma_cm.
In vertical circles, the net centripetal force changes with position because gravity's radial component varies.
The potential energy function U(x) stores energy that can be recovered as kinetic energy.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Forces and Motion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Forces and Motion?

options.text: Any vector can be decomposed into perpendicular components (typically x and y), enabling independent analysis of motion and forces in each direction.
Systematic problem-solving involves drawing free-body diagrams, choosing a coordinate system, applying ΣF = ma in each direction, and solving the resulting system of equations.
Free-body diagrams identify all forces acting on an object, enabling systematic application of F = ma in each coordinate direction.
Work and Energy provide a scalar framework for analyzing motion without tracking forces and accelerations over time.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Forces and Motion as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Forces and Motion?

options.text: Neutron moderation in nuclear reactors: elastic collisions between neutrons and hydrogen atoms (equal mass) transfer maximum kinetic energy in a single collision
Elevator engineering calculating cable tension and motor force requirements for different loading conditions using F = ma for the elevator-passenger system
Spacecraft docking maneuvers calculating the center of mass of the combined vehicle to plan thruster firings for orbit adjustments
Engine crankshaft design optimizing moment of inertia to smooth out power delivery from individual cylinder firings

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Forces and Motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Forces and Motion?

options.text: CD/DVD drive technology computing read speeds at different radii using ω = v/r to maintain constant linear velocity as the read head moves
Designing pulley systems (block and tackle) to provide mechanical advantage for lifting heavy loads in construction
Wind turbine design optimizing blade angular velocity to maximize energy capture while maintaining safe centripetal acceleration at blade tips
Roller coaster design using potential energy diagrams to verify sufficient kinetic energy at all points along the track without external power input

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Forces and Motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Forces and Motion applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Rocket equation derivation: integrating F = dp/dt for variable mass to find the Tsiolkovsky rocket equation Δv = v_e × ln(m₀/m_f)
Analyzing inclined conveyor belt forces in mining operations to determine motor power needed to transport ore uphill
Communication satellite placement in geosynchronous orbit at r ≈ 42,200 km ensuring 24-hour period for continuous coverage of a fixed ground region
Analyzing asteroid capture: determining whether an approaching asteroid's total energy is negative enough to become bound to a planet's gravitational field

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Forces and Motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Forces and Motion?

options.text: Bridge engineering calculating torques about supports to determine forces on beams and ensure structural equilibrium
Spacecraft docking maneuvers calculating the center of mass of the combined vehicle to plan thruster firings for orbit adjustments
Elevator engineering calculating cable tension and motor force requirements for different loading conditions using F = ma for the elevator-passenger system
Foucault pendulum demonstration proving Earth's rotation by observing the slow precession of a long pendulum's swing plane over hours

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Forces and Motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Forces and Motion in practice?

options.text: Orbital mechanics where gravitational force provides the centripetal force: mg = mv²/r leads to orbital velocity v = √(gr) for low orbits
Analyzing braking distance on inclined roads where both gravity component and friction determine deceleration
High jump technique (Fosbury Flop) allows the athlete's center of mass to pass below the bar even as their body clears it
Designing pulley systems (block and tackle) to provide mechanical advantage for lifting heavy loads in construction

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Forces and Motion.
